text: Man. Jeremias had a share in the common misery, (Worthington) and bewails his own condition, as a figure of Christ, Psalm 87:16., and Isaias 53:3. (Calmet)<hr>His disciples must expect to suffer. (Haydock)
Led, or driven me with the rod. (Haydock)<hr>God employs two, Zacharias 11:7. That of rigour was reserved for this prophet; (chap. 38.) none of them suffered more.
Old and wrinkled, the effect of misery.

Gall. Septuagint, "head." Chaldean, "he hath seized the chief," Job 16:13. He speaks in the name of the besieged, who had been threatened with this punishment, ver. 19., and Lamentations 8:14. (Calmet)<hr>And labour. Nabuzardan ransacked the city worse than his master, (Worthington) if the latter was at all present. (Haydock)
Ever indeed, Ecclesiastes 12:5., and Psalm 48:12. Jeremias (Jeremias 38:6.) was in imminent danger.
Prayer. God would not allow him to pray for the people, Lamentations 7:16.
Stones. There is no passage, Job 19:8.
Places. This may refer to the prophet or to the people.
Arrows. Such places were common where shooting was practised, 1 Kings 20:36., and Job 16:11.
Song. True prophets were derided on account of impostors, and because their declarations were unpleasant, etc., Lamentations 17:15., and Ezechiel 12:22.
Wormwood, or a bitter poisonous herb, Lamentations 9:26., and Deuteronomy 29:18.
One. Hebrew, "against a stone." My bread is full of them, Psalm 101:10. (Calmet)<hr>He describes his afflictions, as if his teeth had been broken. (Worthington)
End. Hebrew, "strength." Septuagint, "victory." (Calmet)
Hope. The remembrance fills him with grief and hope, Lamentations 20:12. (Haydock)
New. Novi should be novae, to agree with miserationes. (Calmet)<hr>Chaldean, "new miracles" occur daily. (Haydock)<hr>God's mercies are ever fresh. (Worthington)
Yoke. Afflictions endured for justice sake ensure a blessing. (Haydock)<hr>All may derive great benefit from suffering.
Himself, with perfect resignation.
Hope. He does not doubt, but confides with great humility.
Him. We cannot verify this of the prophet as we can of Christ, (Matthew 26:62.; Calmet) to whom this particularly refers. (Worthington)
Men. He punishes with regret, Ezechiel 18:23. Our crimes force him to chastise, ver. 36. (Calmet)<hr>Yet he seeks our advantage. (Worthington)
Commanded. Hebrew, "said: It cometh to pass," as if by chance. There have always been Epicureans, Ezechiel 8:12., and Psalm 93:7. (Calmet)<hr>Those who deny Providence speak, ver. 30. (Worthington)
Sins? If all happened by inevitable necessity, or according to the laws of Providence, why should any one complain?
Up. This posture "is the testimony of a soul naturally Christian." (Tertullian, Apol.)<hr>Our heart must accompany our hands, 1 Timothy 2:8. (Calmet)
Inexorable. Hebrew and Septuagint, "Thou hast not shewn pity." (Haydock)
Covered thyself, as if not to see our distress.
Cloud. Isaias 59:2., and Ecclesiasticus 35:21. These expressions are admirable.

Prophecy. Many would read, vastatio. Hebrew, "ruin." Septuagint, "taking away." (Calmet)<hr>Protestants, "Fear and a snare is come upon us, desolation and destruction." The prophets were continually in danger. (Haydock)<hr>The preaching of false prophets has brought these evils upon the people. (Worthington)

Wasted. Literally, "robbed." (Haydock)<hr>I have felt more for my people than they have themselves. Moral writers often produce this text, to shew the dangers of an unguarded glance (Calmet) at women.
Over me, as if I were buried. (Haydock)<hr>Jeremias was in prison (chap. 38:6.) when he prayed, ver. 54., and Lamentations 20:7. (Calmet)
Judge what they have judged unjustly. (Worthington)
Lips; or language (Genesis 11:1.) thou knowest, ver. 60.
Up, all their conduct.<hr>Song. Ver. 14.

Buckler, to cover all the body. They shall be surrounded with misery, (Psalm 108:29.) while God will protect his servants, Psalm 5:13. (Calmet)<hr>Let sorrow pierce their heart, as thy enemies are treated. (Worthington)
